title: Glycemic Load Calculator for Meals | Hacé Cuentas
description: Glycemic load = GI × carbs ÷ 100. Measure real blood sugar impact per serving. GL < 10 is low, 11–19 medium, ≥20 high. Free calculator.
intro: The **glycemic load (GL)** was developed in 1997 at Harvard by Walter Willett to fix the main limitation of the glycemic index: GI measures speed but not quantity. GL combines the GI with the actual grams of carbohydrates in your portion: `GL = (GI × carbs in grams) / 100`. Watermelon has a high GI (76) but a 120g serving contains only 6g of carbs, giving GL = 4.5 (low). This calculator asks for the food's GI, portion size in grams, and carbs per 100g, then returns the GL with official classification (low < 10, medium 11–19, high ≥ 20).
keyTakeaway: **GL < 10** is low, **11–19** is medium, **≥ 20** is high. Ideal daily total: **< 100**. More practical than GI alone for meal planning.
useCases: You're diabetic and want to control real blood sugar impact per plate.
You read nutrition labels and want to translate 'grams of carbs' to actual glucose impact.
You're a nutritionist building meal plans with daily GL targets.
You want to compare two recipes with similar carbs but different GI.
You plan to eat fruit and want to know which raises blood sugar least.

fields.label: Carbs per 100g
fields.help: Total carbohydrates per 100g from nutrition label

dataUpdate.notes: Harvard formula (Willett, 1997). Reference table from University of Sydney.

example.title: Example: 150g Plate of White Rice
example.result: A plate of white rice has GL ~31 (high). The same portion of quinoa would give GL ~18.

explanation:
## What is Glycemic Load?

**Glycemic load (GL)** combines GI and the actual amount of carbohydrates in a serving. It was created at Harvard by Walter Willett in 1997.

## Formula

```
GL = (GI × carbs_in_grams) / 100
```

## Classification

| GL | Level |
|---|---|
| < 10 | **Low** |
| 11 – 19 | Medium |
| ≥ 20 | **High** |

**Daily target**: less than 100 points.

## GL of Common Portions

| Food | Serving | GI | Carbs | GL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Watermelon | 120g | 76 | 6 | 4.5 |
| Apple | 150g | 36 | 18 | 6.5 |
| Banana | 120g | 51 | 27 | 13.8 |
| White rice | 150g | 73 | 42 | 30.7 |
| White bread | 30g | 75 | 15 | 11.3 |
| Cooked lentils | 150g | 32 | 30 | 9.6 |
| Pasta | 180g | 49 | 48 | 23.5 |
| Dark chocolate 70% | 25g | 40 | 11 | 4.4 |

## High GI + Small Portion = Low GL

Watermelon is the classic example: GI 76 but GL 4 per serving.

## Recommended Daily GL

- **Type 2 diabetes**: < 120 per day.
- **Weight loss**: < 100.
- **Endurance athletes**: > 150 (to fuel glycogen stores).

## Guidelines

Use this calculator alongside USDA tables for accuracy. Consult a dietitian for a personalized meal plan.

faq.q: What's the difference between glycemic index and glycemic load?
faq.a: GI measures how quickly food raises blood sugar. GL measures the actual blood sugar impact of a real serving by combining GI with carb amount. GL is more practical for meal planning.

faq.q: What's a good daily glycemic load target?
faq.a: Less than 100 for weight management and diabetes control. Endurance athletes may need 150+ to maintain glycogen. Consult your doctor or dietitian for your specific goal.

faq.q: Where do I find carb grams for foods?
faq.a: Check the nutrition label (look for 'total carbohydrate' per 100g) or search the USDA FoodData Central database. Most packaged foods list this.

faq.q: Does fiber count toward glycemic load?
faq.a: Traditionally, GL uses total carbs. However, some people use 'net carbs' (total minus fiber) for a more precise estimate, especially for high-fiber foods.

faq.q: Does pasta have high glycemic load?
faq.a: It depends on portion. A 180g serving has GL ~23 (high), but a smaller 80g serving has GL ~10 (low). Whole wheat pasta is slightly lower than white.

faq.q: Can glycemic load help me lose weight?
faq.a: Yes. Foods and portions with lower GL tend to keep blood sugar stable, reduce hunger spikes, and may help with weight management.

faq.q: What's the glycemic load of fruit?
faq.a: Most whole fruits are low GL per typical serving: apple ~6, banana ~14, orange ~5. The fiber in fruit lowers GL compared to juice.

faq.q: How do I calculate glycemic load if I don't know the exact GI?
faq.a: Look up the food's GI on the official Glycemic Index Foundation database or Harvard's GI tables. If exact numbers aren't available, use estimates from similar foods.
